questions.question: What pointing accuracy is typically required for satellite QKD ground stations?
questions.explanation: Ground stations require sub-arcsecond pointing accuracy to maintain the optical link with a fast-moving LEO satellite. This is typically achieved through beacon-based closed-loop tracking.

questions.question: Why are SNSPDs preferred over SPADs for satellite QKD detection?

options.text: Higher efficiency, lower dark counts, better timing

questions.explanation: SNSPDs offer ~90% efficiency (vs ~60% for SPADs), dark counts of ~10 Hz (vs ~100 Hz), and timing jitter of ~50 ps (vs ~350 ps). The trade-off is requiring cryogenic cooling to ~2.5K.

questions.question: What is the purpose of spectral filtering in ground stations?

options.text: To reduce background light and noise

questions.explanation: Spectral filtering uses narrowband filters (~1 nm bandwidth) to reject background light (sunlight, moonlight, city lights) while passing the QKD signal photons, improving signal-to-noise ratio.

questions.question: What tracking method provides the best pointing accuracy?
questions.explanation: Closed-loop beacon tracking uses a bright beacon from the satellite, tracked by a camera on the ground station, with real-time feedback to the mount. This achieves sub-arcsecond accuracy despite atmospheric effects.

questions.question: Why is site altitude important for ground stations?

options.text: Satellites are easier to see from high altitude
Higher altitude reduces the atmospheric path length

questions.explanation: Higher altitude sites have less atmosphere above them, reducing both absorption losses and turbulence effects. Sites above 2000m can significantly reduce atmospheric losses compared to sea level.

questions.question: What is 'seeing' in the context of ground stations?
questions.explanation: Seeing measures atmospheric turbulence that causes star images to blur and dance. Good seeing (<1 arcsec) is important for maintaining tight beam focus and efficient fiber coupling.

questions.question: Ground stations for satellite QKD typically need telescopes with apertures of several meters diameter.
questions.explanation: False. Ground station telescopes typically have apertures of 0.5-1.0 meters, not several meters. Larger apertures collect more photons but have diminishing returns due to atmospheric turbulence effects.
